    21→## 4. Namespace
    22→

    26→
    27→## 5. Custom Definitions
    28→
    29→N/A — no custom `def`, `abbrev`, or `structure` declarations in this file.
    30→
    31→## 6. Theorem Statements
    32→
    33→- [x] Benchmark problems use the `theorem` keyword.
    34→- [x] Main theorem is named `erdos_641` (snake_case, matching namespace number).
    35→- [x] Names follow Lean/Mathlib `snake_case` conventions for Props.
    36→- [x] Each theorem has both `@[category ...]` and `@[AMS ...]` on the **same attribute line** (e.g., `@[category research solved, AMS 5]`).
    37→- [x] Main theorem has a docstring (`/-- ... -/`).
    38→- [x] Docstrings reference source papers: `[Er92b]`, `[Er97d, p.84]`, `[JSS24]`.
    39→
    40→### `answer()` Usage
    41→
    42→The original problem asks: *"Is there some function f such that..."* — this is a yes/no question (**Case 1**).
    43→
    44→- [x] The theorem type is `answer(False) ↔ P`. Since the conjecture was disproved (Janzer–Steiner–Sudakov), `answer(False)` is correct.
    45→- [x] The RHS `P` formalizes the question as originally asked: for all `k ≥ 1`, there exists a threshold `f` such that any finite graph with chromatic number `≥ f` has `k` edge-disjoint cycles on the same vertex set. `answer(True)` would mean "yes" and `answer(False)` means "no" to that question.
    46→- [x] Quantifiers go **after** `answer(False)`, not as theorem parameters.
    47→
    48→**Note on quantifier order:** The original problem asks for a single function `f : ℕ → ℕ`, while the formalization uses `∀ k, ∃ f : ℕ` (a potentially different threshold for each `k`). These are logically equivalent via the axiom of choice (available in Lean/Mathlib), so this is a valid encoding.
    49→
    50→## 7. Category Attribute
    51→
    52→- [x] Every theorem has exactly one `@[category ...]`.
    53→- [x] Value is valid: `research solved` (the conjecture was disproved by JSS24, but not formally verified in Lean).
    54→- [x] Not marked `research open` — consistent with having been resolved.
    55→
    56→## 8. AMS Attribute
    57→
    58→- [x] Every theorem has exactly one `@[AMS ...]` attribute.
    59→- [x] At least one AMS subject number listed: `5` (combinatorics).
    60→- [x] Numbers in ascending order (trivially, only one number).
    61→- [x] No duplicates.
    62→- [x] `5` is a valid AMS MSC2020 code.
    63→
    64→## 9. Proof Bodies
    65→
    66→- [x] Both theorems use multi-line `by\n  sorry` format — acceptable.
    67→- [x] No `sorry` in definitions (no definitions present).
    68→- [x] No informal proof sketches for solved problems.
    69→- [x] No `native_decide`.
    70→
    71→## 10. Variants & Multi-part Problems
    72→
    73→- [x] Variant named `erdos_641.variants.disproof` — correct naming convention.
    74→- [x] Variant lives in the **same file** as the main theorem.
    75→- [x] Variant has its own `@[category research solved, AMS 5]` attributes.
    76→- [x] Variant has a docstring explaining it formalizes the negation/disproof direction.
    77→
    78→**Note:** The variant correctly formalizes the existential form of the disproof: there exists `k ≥ 1` (namely `k = 2`) such that no threshold `f` suffices. This is the logical negation of the main statement and accurately reflects the JSS24 result.
    79→
    80→## 11. Code Quality
    81→
    82→- [x] 2-space indentation throughout.
    83→- [x] Unicode math symbols used: `∀`, `∃`, `≤`, `¬`, `↔`, `ℕ`, `ℕ∞`.
    84→- [x] No debug code (`#check`, `#eval`, `#print`, `dbg_trace`).
    85→- [x] No `set_option` disabling linters.
    86→- [x] Lines are reasonably short (all under 100 characters).
    87→- [x] File ends with a single trailing newline.
    88→
    89→## 12. Build Verification
    90→
    91→- [ ] `lake build` — not verified in this review.
    92→- [ ] Custom linters — not verified in this review.
    93→
    94→---
    95→
    96→## Summary
    97→
    98→**Result: PASS** (modulo build verification)
    99→
   100→The file is well-structured and conforms to all stylistic and structural requirements of the checklist. The formalization correctly captures the yes/no nature of Problem 641 using `answer(False) ↔ P` (Case 1), reflecting its disproof by Janzer, Steiner, and Sudakov. The variant cleanly formalizes the existential negation. The quantifier-order choice (`∀ k, ∃ f` vs. `∃ f, ∀ k`) is equivalent via choice and is mathematically sound. Both the main theorem and variant are properly documented with references and correctly categorized as `research solved`.

file.content:
# Review: `deepmind/641.lean` — Erdős Problem 641

## 1. Copyright Header

- [x] File begins with the **exact** copyright block (no leading blank line).
- [x] Year is exactly `2026`.

## 2. Import Statement

- [x] Exactly one import line, immediately after the copyright block (separated by one blank line): `import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports` (line 17).
- [x] No other imports are present.

## 3. Module Docstring

- [x] A `/-! ... -/` module docstring appears after the import, separated by one blank line (lines 19–31).
- [x] Starts with heading: `# Erdős Problem 641`.
- [x] Contains a `*Reference:*` line with a markdown link: `[erdosproblems.com/641](https://www.erdosproblems.com/641)`.
- [x] Includes a brief plain-English description of the problem (line 24).
- [x] Bibliography entries present: `[Er92b]`, `[Er97d]`, `[JSS24]` (lines 26–30).

## 4. Namespace

- [x] All declarations are inside `namespace Erdos641 ... end Erdos641`.
- [x] Namespace name uses `UpperCamelCase` (`Erdos641`).
- [x] `open SimpleGraph` appears **before** the namespace block (line 33).

## 5. Custom Definitions

N/A — no custom `def`, `abbrev`, or `structure` declarations in this file.

## 6. Theorem Statements

- [x] Benchmark problems use the `theorem` keyword.
- [x] Main theorem is named `erdos_641` (snake_case, matching namespace number).
- [x] Names follow Lean/Mathlib `snake_case` conventions for Props.
- [x] Each theorem has both `@[category ...]` and `@[AMS ...]` on the **same attribute line** (e.g., `@[category research solved, AMS 5]`).
- [x] Main theorem has a docstring (`/-- ... -/`).
- [x] Docstrings reference source papers: `[Er92b]`, `[Er97d, p.84]`, `[JSS24]`.

### `answer()` Usage

The original problem asks: *"Is there some function f such that..."* — this is a yes/no question (**Case 1**).

- [x] The theorem type is `answer(False) ↔ P`. Since the conjecture was disproved (Janzer–Steiner–Sudakov), `answer(False)` is correct.
- [x] The RHS `P` formalizes the question as originally asked: for all `k ≥ 1`, there exists a threshold `f` such that any finite graph with chromatic number `≥ f` has `k` edge-disjoint cycles on the same vertex set. `answer(True)` would mean "yes" and `answer(False)` means "no" to that question.
- [x] Quantifiers go **after** `answer(False)`, not as theorem parameters.

**Note on quantifier order:** The original problem asks for a single function `f : ℕ → ℕ`, while the formalization uses `∀ k, ∃ f : ℕ` (a potentially different threshold for each `k`). These are logically equivalent via the axiom of choice (available in Lean/Mathlib), so this is a valid encoding.

## 7. Category Attribute

- [x] Every theorem has exactly one `@[category ...]`.
- [x] Value is valid: `research solved` (the conjecture was disproved by JSS24, but not formally verified in Lean).
- [x] Not marked `research open` — consistent with having been resolved.

## 8. AMS Attribute

- [x] Every theorem has exactly one `@[AMS ...]` attribute.
- [x] At least one AMS subject number listed: `5` (combinatorics).
- [x] Numbers in ascending order (trivially, only one number).
- [x] No duplicates.
- [x] `5` is a valid AMS MSC2020 code.

## 9. Proof Bodies

- [x] Both theorems use multi-line `by\n  sorry` format — acceptable.
- [x] No `sorry` in definitions (no definitions present).
- [x] No informal proof sketches for solved problems.
- [x] No `native_decide`.

## 10. Variants & Multi-part Problems

- [x] Variant named `erdos_641.variants.disproof` — correct naming convention.
- [x] Variant lives in the **same file** as the main theorem.
- [x] Variant has its own `@[category research solved, AMS 5]` attributes.
- [x] Variant has a docstring explaining it formalizes the negation/disproof direction.

**Note:** The variant correctly formalizes the existential form of the disproof: there exists `k ≥ 1` (namely `k = 2`) such that no threshold `f` suffices. This is the logical negation of the main statement and accurately reflects the JSS24 result.

## 11. Code Quality

- [x] 2-space indentation throughout.
- [x] Unicode math symbols used: `∀`, `∃`, `≤`, `¬`, `↔`, `ℕ`, `ℕ∞`.
- [x] No debug code (`#check`, `#eval`, `#print`, `dbg_trace`).
- [x] No `set_option` disabling linters.
- [x] Lines are reasonably short (all under 100 characters).
- [x] File ends with a single trailing newline.

## 12. Build Verification

- [ ] `lake build` — not verified in this review.
- [ ] Custom linters — not verified in this review.

---

## Summary

**Result: PASS** (modulo build verification)

The file is well-structured and conforms to all stylistic and structural requirements of the checklist. The formalization correctly captures the yes/no nature of Problem 641 using `answer(False) ↔ P` (Case 1), reflecting its disproof by Janzer, Steiner, and Sudakov. The variant cleanly formalizes the existential negation. The quantifier-order choice (`∀ k, ∃ f` vs. `∃ f, ∀ k`) is equivalent via choice and is mathematically sound. Both the main theorem and variant are properly documented with references and correctly categorized as `research solved`.

content.text: The review passed (**Result: PASS**). No changes needed.
